notes: That night at dinner, Esther revealed the plot against the Jews and Haman's part in it. The king, angered, left the room, and upon his return found Haman groveling before the queen, perhaps even touching her, and immediately ordered Haman hanged on the very gallows he had built for Mordecai (Esth. 6:14-7:10). Because he could not reverse the royal edict permitting the persecution of the Jews, the king issued another edict permitting the Jews to defend themselves (Esth. 8:1-17). On the 14th and 15th of Adar (the last month of the Jewish calendar), the Jews killed as many as seventy-five thousand Persians in response to attacks (Esth. 9:1-19). This caused a great celebration, which is still observed today, known as Purim (Esth. 9:20-32). The book of Esther ends with a final note on the greatness of Mordecai and the work that he did for his people (Esth. 10:1-3). (Roger Keller in Kent P. Jackson, ed., Studies in Scripture, Vol. 4: 1 Kings to Malachi, p.388)

on which the Persians reclined at table. But it is probable that they were not very different from those used by the Greeks and Romans. Haman, perhaps, at first stood up to beg pardon of Esther; but driven in his extremity to resort to an attitude of the most earnest supplication, he fell prostrate on the couch where the queen was recumbent. The king returning that instant was fired at what seemed an outrage on female modesty.

"The king imagines in his absence Haman wants to commit an indecent assault on Esther. And his wrath increases. That is the moment when Harbonah, the chamberlain, interferes. He points at the gallows-tree … Haman is then hanged on that tree" (Friedrich Weinreb, Chance the Hidden Lord - The Amazing Scroll of Esther, 150).

they covered Haman's face--The import of this striking action is, that a criminal is unworthy any longer to look on the face of the king, and hence, when criminals are consigned to their doom in Persia, the first thing is to cover the face with a veil or napkin.

Harbonah, one of the chamberlains, said before the king, Behold also, the gallows--This eunuch had probably been the messenger sent with the invitation to Haman, and on that occasion had seen the gallows. The information he now volunteered, as well it may be from abhorrence of Haman's cold-blooded conspiracy as from sympathy with his amiable mistress, involved with her people in imminent peril.
